dataset.name: EID Quick Reference - Active Directory Domain Services

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Active Directory replication request denied
entries.summary: A replication request to this domain controller was denied.
entries.details: Generated when a domain controller denies an inbound replication request. Key fields: SourceDC (the machine requesting replication) and the reason for denial. In DFIR contexts this event is relevant as a failed DCSync indicator: DCSync (T1003.006) works by mimicking domain controller replication using DRSUAPI calls (GetNCChanges). A non-DC machine requesting replication will be denied and may generate this event. However, note that a successful DCSync attack will NOT generate this event — it will be visible in Security log EID 4662 (Directory Service Object Access with the required replication permissions). This event primarily captures failed or unauthorized replication attempts. SourceDC that is not a known DC is a critical indicator. DC-only event.

investigationPivots: SourceDC that is not a known domain controller in the environment is a critical DCSync attempt indicator
Correlate with Security log EID 4662 on the DC for successful replication permission use — absence of denial + presence of 4662 with replication permissions indicates a successful DCSync
Investigate the source machine immediately if it is not a registered DC
commonFalsePositives: Decommissioned DCs that were not cleanly removed from AD attempting to replicate
Azure AD Connect or third-party directory tools that use replication APIs for read-only sync

entries.title: LDAP search statistics — expensive query threshold exceeded
entries.summary: A domain controller recorded statistics for an LDAP search that exceeded configured thresholds.
entries.details: Generated on domain controllers when an LDAP search exceeds configured thresholds for visited entries, returned entries, or elapsed time. Key fields: IPAddress (client performing the search), AccountName, SearchFilter, StartingNode (the base DN), and statistics for VisitedEntries, ReturnedEntries, and TimeElapsed. This event is the primary detection mechanism for LDAP-based Active Directory enumeration by tools such as BloodHound, SharpHound, ADFind, and ldapsearch. Adversaries conducting reconnaissance (T1018, T1069) perform broad LDAP queries to enumerate users, groups, computers, GPOs, trusts, and ACLs. A single IP issuing many EID 1644 events with large VisitedEntries counts within a short window is a high-confidence indicator of AD recon. Requires enabling via registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NTDS\Diagnostics\15 Field Engineering = 5. DC-only event.

prerequisites.description: Enable LDAP expensive query logging by setting HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NTDS\Diagnostics\15 Field Engineering = 5. Optionally configure search time and result thresholds in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NTDS\Parameters.

investigationPivots: IPAddress generating many EID 1644 events in rapid succession is the primary BloodHound/SharpHound indicator
SearchFilter containing '(objectClass=*)' or '(objectCategory=*)' on the root DC or domain DN indicates a full AD dump query
AccountName reveals the account performing recon — service accounts performing broad LDAP queries are anomalous
Correlate IPAddress with the machine's normal role — workstations performing DC LDAP queries are more suspicious than servers
commonFalsePositives: Directory synchronization tools (Azure AD Connect, LDAP sync agents) that perform large, periodic LDAP queries
IT management software that enumerates AD for asset discovery
Threshold set too low — adjust NTDS diagnostics threshold to reduce false positive volume

entries.title: LDAP server signing requirements not enforced
entries.summary: The LDAP server is not configured to require signing or sealing on all connections.
entries.details: Generated when the Active Directory LDAP server detects that signing or sealing is not required for all incoming LDAP connections. This is a configuration event fired at DC startup or when the LDAP signing policy is checked. LDAP connections without signing are vulnerable to relay attacks — an attacker who captures LDAP authentication can relay it to the DC to perform operations as the victim. This is a posture event rather than an attack event: it indicates the DC is susceptible to LDAP relay. The remediation is to set the 'Domain controller: LDAP server signing requirements' Group Policy to 'Require signing'. DC-only event.

investigationPivots: Confirm current LDAP signing policy via Group Policy and registry (HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\NTDS\Parameters\LDAPServerIntegrity)
Presence of this event indicates unsigned LDAP connections are permitted — cross-reference with EID 2887 summary to assess volume
Remediate by setting the DC LDAP signing requirement to 'Require signing' after validating client compatibility
commonFalsePositives: This event fires on DCs where LDAP signing is intentionally not enforced due to legacy application requirements
Environments with older LDAP clients that do not support signing

entries.title: LDAP unsigned bind summary
entries.summary: Hourly summary of LDAP connections that were made without signing or sealing.
entries.details: Generated approximately once per hour on domain controllers to report a summary count of LDAP binds received that did not use signing or sealing (LDAP Simple Binds or unsigned SASL binds). Key fields: NumberOfUnsignedBinds (total unsigned binds in the period). This event is a periodic summary companion to EID 2889, which fires per individual occurrence. When the count is non-zero, clients are authenticating to the DC without LDAP signing — these connections are vulnerable to relay attacks and credential interception. A sustained high count may indicate a legacy application or an adversary actively exploiting unsigned LDAP to relay credentials. Use EID 2889 to identify the specific clients. DC-only event.

investigationPivots: A non-zero count should trigger review of EID 2889 for individual client identification
Track the count over time — a sudden spike may indicate a new application or active attack rather than a known legacy client
Use EID 2889 client IP addresses to identify and remediate unsigned LDAP sources
commonFalsePositives: Legacy applications that perform LDAP Simple Binds and have not been updated to support signing
Third-party directory integration tools that use unsigned LDAP for compatibility

entries.title: Client performed an unsigned LDAP bind
entries.summary: A client made an LDAP Simple Bind or unsigned SASL bind to this domain controller.
entries.details: Generated per occurrence when a client performs an LDAP bind without signing or sealing — either a Simple Bind (credentials sent in plaintext or over an unprotected channel) or an unsigned SASL bind. Key fields: IPAddress (the client making the unsigned bind) and AccountName (the account used). LDAP Simple Binds transmit credentials without encryption or integrity protection, making them susceptible to interception. Adversaries performing LDAP relay attacks will appear here if they are forwarding captured LDAP authentication to the DC. A new, unknown IPAddress making Simple Binds with privileged AccountName values is a high-confidence relay indicator. Requires that the NTDS 'LDAP Interface Events' diagnostic key be set to 2 or higher. DC-only event.

prerequisites.description: Enable LDAP Interface Events logging by setting HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NTDS\Diagnostics\16 LDAP Interface Events = 2 (or higher).

investigationPivots: IPAddress is the primary pivot — identify the machine and determine if it is a known legacy application host
AccountName for a privileged account (Domain Admin, service account with broad access) making a Simple Bind is highest priority
A new IPAddress making Simple Binds that was not present in prior EID 2887 summaries may indicate an attacker-controlled relay host
commonFalsePositives: Known legacy applications that perform Simple Binds as a long-standing configuration
Third-party directory sync or authentication proxy tools using Simple Binds for compatibility

entries.title: An operation was performed on an Active Directory object
entries.summary: A security-relevant operation was performed on a directory service object.
entries.details: Generated when an operation (read, write, or extended right) is performed on an Active Directory object with auditing enabled on that object. Key fields: SubjectAccountName (who performed the operation), ObjectDN (the full distinguished name of the target object), ObjectClass (e.g., domainDNS, user, group), Properties (attributes accessed), and AccessMask. This event is the primary mechanism for detecting DCSync attacks: a successful DCSync will generate EID 4662 on the domain controller with ObjectClass=domainDNS and extended rights access including Replicating Directory Changes (1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2) and Replicating Directory Changes All (1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2). SubjectAccountName for a non-DC machine account making replication right calls is a DCSync indicator. Also used to audit AdminSDHolder modifications and ACL changes on sensitive objects. Requires configuring SACL (System Access Control List) auditing on the target AD objects. DC-only event.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit Directory Service Access' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration. Additionally, configure SACLs on target AD objects (e.g., domain root object) to audit the desired operations.

investigationPivots: Filter for AccessMask containing the Replicating Directory Changes GUIDs (1131f6aa and 1131f6ad) — these are necessary for DCSync
SubjectAccountName that is not a domain controller machine account (ending in $) requesting replication rights on domainDNS objects is a high-confidence DCSync indicator
Correlate SubjectAccountName with EID 4624 logon events to identify the originating host and session
For AdminSDHolder abuse, look for 4662 on CN=AdminSDHolder,CN=System with write/DACL access from non-standard accounts
ObjectDN targeting the domain root or krbtgt object combined with replication rights indicates potential Golden/Silver Ticket preparation
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate domain controller machine accounts performing normal AD replication will generate these events continuously
Azure AD Connect and third-party directory sync tools granted replication permissions will appear regularly
Privileged admin tools that read sensitive AD object attributes during normal operation

keyFields.name: SubjectUserName
keyFields.description: Account that performed the access; non-DC machine accounts performing replication are DCSync indicators

keyFields.name: ObjectName
keyFields.description: Distinguished name of the accessed object; domain root (DC=domain,DC=com) is the DCSync target

keyFields.name: Properties
keyFields.description: GUIDs of accessed properties; 1131f6ad (DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All) = DCSync critical indicator

keyFields.name: AccessMask
keyFields.description: Access rights requested; 0x100 (Control Access) is used in DCSync operations

keyFields.name: OperationType
keyFields.description: Type of operation performed on the object

detectionRules.title: DCSync — Replication Rights Accessed by Non-DC Account
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4662
| where AccessMask == "0x100"
| where Properties has "1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2"
    or Properties has "1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2"
    or Properties has "89e95b76-444d-4c62-991a-0facbeda640c"
| where SubjectUserName !endswith "$"
| project TimeGenerated, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName, ObjectName, Properties, Computer
detectionRules.notes: GUIDs: 1131f6ad = DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All, 1131f6aa = DS-Replication-Get-Changes, 89e95b76 = DS-Replication-Get-Changes-In-Filtered-Set. Non-machine-account subjects are high confidence DCSync.

detectionRules.title: DCSync Detection via AD Object Access
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4662 Access_Mask=0x100
(Properties="*1131f6ad*" OR Properties="*1131f6aa*" OR Properties="*89e95b76*")
NOT Account_Name="*$"
| table _time, Account_Name, Object_Name, Properties, host
detectionRules.notes: Identifies non-computer accounts accessing replication GUIDs on domain objects.

detectionRules.title: Active Directory DCSync Attack
detectionRules.rule:
title: Active Directory DCSync Attack
status: stable
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4662
        AccessMask: '0x100'
        Properties|contains:
            - '1131f6ad'
            - '1131f6aa'
            - '89e95b76'
    filter_machine:
        SubjectUserName|endswith: '$'
    condition: selection and not filter_machine
falsepositives:
    - Legitimate AD replication between domain controllers
level: high
detectionRules.notes: Well-established detection for Mimikatz dcsync and similar tools.

entries.title: A new trust was created to a domain
entries.summary: A new trust relationship was established between domains or forests.
entries.details: Generated when a new trust relationship is created to another domain or forest. Key fields: DomainName (the trusted domain), TrustType (Windows domain trust vs. MIT Kerberos), TrustDirection (inbound, outbound, or bidirectional), and TrustAttributes (e.g., forest-transitive, cross-organization). Trust relationships fundamentally alter the AD security boundary — an attacker with Domain Admin privileges can create a new trust to an attacker-controlled domain to establish a persistent, stealthy access path. Bidirectional trusts or forest-transitive trusts with attacker-controlled domains can enable cross-domain credential abuse and lateral movement that persists even after password resets. Creation of unexpected trusts is a high-severity persistence indicator. DC-only event.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Trust Modification

investigationPivots: Any new trust to a domain not in the known-good trust inventory should be treated as critical and investigated immediately
DomainName that is external or unrecognized is an immediate escalation trigger
Correlate with SubjectAccountName — only Domain Admins or Enterprise Admins should be creating trusts
TrustDirection = Bidirectional or TrustAttributes = forest-transitive elevates severity significantly
commonFalsePositives: Planned infrastructure changes such as mergers, acquisitions, or new domain deployments by authorized admins
Automated provisioning scripts that establish trusts as part of domain build processes

entries.title: A trust to a domain was removed
entries.summary: An existing trust relationship to a domain was deleted.
entries.details: Generated when a trust relationship to another domain or forest is removed. Key fields: DomainName (the formerly trusted domain) and SubjectAccountName (who removed the trust). Trust removal can be both an attacker action and a defensive action — an attacker may remove a legitimate trust to create disruption or to mask a previously created malicious trust. In incident response, removal of the legitimate trust after creating a backdoor trust is a known anti-forensics tactic. Any unplanned or unauthorized trust removal should be investigated, particularly if paired temporally with EID 4706 (new trust creation). DC-only event.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Trust Modification

investigationPivots: Unscheduled trust removal, especially outside change windows, is a high-priority alert
Temporal correlation with EID 4706 (trust created) may indicate a trust swap attack — old trust removed, malicious trust added
SubjectAccountName should only be Domain Admins or Enterprise Admins; any other account is highly anomalous
commonFalsePositives: Decommissioning of retired domains as part of planned infrastructure cleanup
Post-acquisition integration that removes redundant trusts after domain consolidation

entries.title: Kerberos policy was changed
entries.summary: The Kerberos authentication policy for the domain was modified.
entries.details: Generated when the Kerberos ticket policy for the domain is changed, including the maximum lifetime for user tickets (TGT lifetime), service tickets (TGS lifetime), ticket renewal lifetime, and clock skew tolerance. Key fields: SubjectAccountName (who made the change) and the before/after values for each policy parameter. Adversaries with domain admin access may extend TGT lifetimes to reduce Golden Ticket detection windows (longer lifetimes mean valid tickets persist for longer), or extend renewal periods to reduce authentication noise. In Golden Ticket attacks, extending the MaxTicketAge reduces the need for the attacker to re-acquire credentials. Any change to default Kerberos policy (TGT = 10 hours, service ticket = 10 hours, renewal = 7 days) without a change ticket is suspicious. DC-only event.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification

investigationPivots: Compare new values against baseline — increases to MaxTicketAge or MaxRenewAge beyond defaults (10h TGT, 7 day renewal) are suspicious
SubjectAccountName should be a known admin; changes by service accounts or unfamiliar users require investigation
Correlate with other AD policy change events (EID 4739) to determine scope of domain-wide policy modification
commonFalsePositives: Planned Kerberos hardening changes (e.g., reducing TGT lifetime to align with STIG/CIS benchmarks)
Domain migration or consolidation activities requiring temporary policy adjustments

entries.title: Trusted domain information was modified
entries.summary: The attributes of an existing trust relationship were changed.
entries.details: Generated when an existing trust relationship is modified — for example, changing trust direction, trust type, trust attributes, or SID filtering settings. Key fields: DomainName (the trusted domain affected), and the old vs. new values of the trust attributes. SID filtering (also called quarantine) prevents SIDs from a trusted domain from being used to elevate privileges in the trusting domain. Attackers with domain admin access who want to enable SID History-based privilege escalation across a trust may disable SID filtering (set TrustAttributes to TREAT_AS_EXTERNAL or remove the QUARANTINE_DOMAIN flag). This is a high-value event for detecting cross-domain privilege escalation setup. DC-only event.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Trust Modification

investigationPivots: Modification that removes the QUARANTINE_DOMAIN flag (SID filtering) from a trust is a critical escalation indicator
Changes to trust direction from outbound to bidirectional are high priority — they expand the attack surface
SubjectAccountName should be a Domain Admin or Enterprise Admin; changes by other accounts require immediate investigation
commonFalsePositives: Planned changes to trust attributes during domain consolidation or forest restructuring
Administrative adjustments to enable specific authentication flows for application compatibility

entries.title: Domain Policy was changed
entries.summary: The domain policy (password policy, lockout policy, or Kerberos policy) was modified.
entries.details: Generated when changes are made to the Default Domain Policy affecting password policy, account lockout policy, or Kerberos policy at the domain level. Key fields: SubjectAccountName (who made the change), DomainName, and the specific policy values modified. Adversaries with domain admin access may weaken the password policy (e.g., remove minimum length, disable complexity) to enable easier brute-force of newly set passwords, or increase the lockout threshold to allow more password spray attempts without triggering lockouts. Attackers may also extend the maximum password age to prevent forced password rotations that would disrupt compromised accounts. Any decrease in security posture of these policies is a high-severity finding. DC-only event.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification

investigationPivots: Compare changed values against the known baseline — any reduction in password minimum length, complexity, or increase in lockout threshold is suspicious
Removal of account lockout (LockoutThreshold = 0) combined with password spray attempts (EID 4625 bursts) is a high-confidence attack indicator
SubjectAccountName should be a known, authorized admin; automated change management tools should be the only non-interactive sources
commonFalsePositives: Planned compliance-driven changes to bring policy in line with NIST 800-63 or CIS benchmarks
Emergency changes to lockout policy during an incident to prevent widespread account lockout from a spray attack

entries.title: SID History was added to an account
entries.summary: A SID was successfully added to the SID History attribute of an account.
entries.details: Generated when a SID is successfully added to the SIDHistory attribute of a user or group object. Key fields: TargetAccountName (the account receiving the SID History entry), TargetDomainName, SubjectAccountName (who performed the action), and OldSidHistory/NewSidHistory (before and after values). SID History is a legitimate AD attribute designed to preserve access rights during domain migrations — when accounts are migrated from an old domain, the old SID is stored in SID History so existing ACLs still grant access. Adversaries abuse SID History to grant an account the effective privileges of any SID, including Enterprise Admin or Domain Admin SIDs from the current or a trusted domain, without visibly adding the account to those groups. This is a high-fidelity persistence indicator when seen outside a documented domain migration. Requires Audit Account Management policy.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit Account Management' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > Account Management.

investigationPivots: Any SID History addition outside a documented domain migration window should be treated as a critical incident
Check NewSidHistory for high-privilege SIDs — Domain Admins (S-1-5-21-...-512), Enterprise Admins (S-1-5-21-...-519), or krbtgt SIDs are highest severity
SubjectAccountName reveals who performed the injection — should only be a migration admin or authorized script
Correlate TargetAccountName with subsequent privileged access (EID 4624 + 4672) to confirm exploitation
commonFalsePositives: Authorized domain migrations using ADMT (Active Directory Migration Tool) or similar tooling
Post-acquisition domain consolidation where accounts are migrated and retain SID History for ACL compatibility

entries.title: An attempt to add SID History to an account failed
entries.summary: An attempt to add a SID to an account's SID History attribute was denied.
entries.details: Generated when an attempt to add a SID to the SIDHistory attribute of an account is rejected. Key fields: TargetAccountName (the account targeted), SubjectAccountName (who attempted the operation), and the failure reason. SID History modification failures can occur because the caller lacks the required privileges, or because the DC's security settings prevent SID History injection (e.g., when SID filtering is enabled on the trust). In an attack scenario, this event may indicate an adversary attempting to inject privileged SIDs into a user account and being blocked by DC-level controls. While less critical than a successful EID 4765, repeated failures targeting privileged SIDs from the same source indicate active SID injection attempts.

investigationPivots: Repeated failures targeting the same TargetAccountName from the same SubjectAccountName indicate an active attack in progress
Correlate with EID 4716 (trust modification) — an attacker may be attempting to disable SID filtering before retrying the injection
SubjectAccountName that is not a known migration admin is highly anomalous
commonFalsePositives: Domain migration tools encountering permission issues during automated account migration
Misconfigured migration scripts lacking the required SeNetworkLogonRight or replication privileges

entries.title: An Active Directory replica source naming context was established
entries.summary: A replication source naming context was established between two domain controllers.
entries.details: Generated when a domain controller establishes a new replication source for a naming context (NC). Key fields: DestinationDRA (the DC receiving replication), SourceDRA (the DC providing replication), NamingContext (the AD partition being replicated, e.g., DC=corp,DC=com), and Options. This event fires during legitimate DC-to-DC replication setup and is primarily useful for baselining the normal replication topology. In attack scenarios, a new unexpected source DC in the replication topology may indicate a rogue DC has been joined or that a DCSync source is masquerading as a DC. Correlate with EID 4932/4933 (sync begin/end) to understand replication activity. DC-only event.

investigationPivots: SourceDRA that is not in the known DC inventory is a critical indicator — check against registered DCs in Sites and Services
Unexpected NamingContext partitions (e.g., Schema or Configuration NC being requested by an unknown source) are anomalous
Correlate with EID 4932/4933 on the same DC to see if the established source was used for a sync
commonFalsePositives: Normal replication topology establishment during DC promotion or decommission
Adding a new DC to an existing site triggers legitimate source NC establishment events

entries.title: An Active Directory replica source naming context was removed
entries.summary: A replication source naming context was removed from this domain controller.
entries.details: Generated when a replication source naming context is removed — typically during DC decommission, site link changes, or replication topology updates. Key fields: DestinationDRA, SourceDRA, NamingContext, and Options. This event is most useful for detecting cleanup after a DCSync attack or for tracking unauthorized changes to the replication topology. An attacker who established a rogue DC for DCSync purposes may trigger this event when removing it after credential extraction. Sudden removal of replication sources without a corresponding change management record is worth investigating. DC-only event.

investigationPivots: Removal of a SourceDRA that was not in the original known-good baseline may indicate a transient rogue DC was cleaned up
Correlate temporally with EID 4928 for the same SourceDRA — short-lived establishment followed by removal is a DCSync indicator
Check ADSI Edit or repadmin /showrepl to confirm current replication topology matches expected state
commonFalsePositives: DC decommissioning as part of planned infrastructure changes
Replication topology changes due to site link cost adjustments or AD Sites and Services reconfiguration

entries.title: An Active Directory replica source naming context was modified
entries.summary: The attributes of a replication source naming context were changed.
entries.details: Generated when the parameters of an existing replication source naming context are modified — for example, changes to replication options, schedule, or transport. Key fields: DestinationDRA, SourceDRA, NamingContext, Options (before/after). While typically a benign administrative event, unexpected modifications to replication source attributes may indicate tampering with the replication topology to redirect data flows or suppress audit events. DC-only event.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification

investigationPivots: Unexpected changes to Options flags (e.g., disabling notifications or changing schedules) without change tickets are suspicious
Correlate with SubjectAccountName — only Domain Admins or the SYSTEM account of the DC itself should modify replication sources
Use repadmin /showrepl and repadmin /replsummary to validate that the current topology matches expected state
commonFalsePositives: Replication topology adjustments by AD administrators tuning replication schedules or costs
Automated configuration management tools modifying replication settings as part of DC build processes

entries.title: Synchronization of a replica of an AD naming context has begun
entries.summary: A domain controller has started synchronizing a naming context from a source DC.
entries.details: Generated at the start of an Active Directory naming context (partition) synchronization between DCs. Key fields: DestinationDRA (the DC receiving the sync), SourceDRA (the DC providing the data), NamingContext (the partition being synced, e.g., the domain NC or Schema NC), and Options. EID 4932 marks the start and EID 4933 marks the end of a sync cycle. In DCSync detection, a successful Mimikatz/Impacket DCSync attack results in GetNCChanges DRSUAPI calls that trigger both 4932 and 4933 on the target DC, with SourceDRA being the attacker's machine (not a registered DC). The combination of SourceDRA that is not a known DC plus a short sync duration (4932 to 4933 gap of seconds) is a high-fidelity DCSync indicator. Correlate with EID 4662 for the replication rights access check. DC-only event.

investigationPivots: SourceDRA that is not a registered DC (check against known DC list or AD Sites and Services) is the primary DCSync indicator
Short elapsed time between EID 4932 and EID 4933 (seconds rather than minutes) combined with an unknown SourceDRA is high-confidence DCSync
NamingContext targeting the domain NC (DC=corp,DC=com) is expected in DCSync; Schema or Configuration NC is unusual
Correlate SourceDRA hostname/IP with EID 4624 logon events to identify the originating account and workstation
commonFalsePositives: Normal DC-to-DC replication events between registered domain controllers — high volume expected in healthy AD environments
Azure AD Connect with replication rights will generate these events periodically
RODC (Read-Only DC) synchronization from a writable DC

keyFields.name: DestinationDRA
keyFields.description: Distinguished name of the destination DC; unexpected non-DC names indicate rogue replication

keyFields.name: NamingContext
keyFields.description: Naming context being replicated (domain, configuration, schema); domain NC is most sensitive

keyFields.name: Options
keyFields.description: Replication options bitmask

keyFields.name: SessionOperation
keyFields.description: Operation type within the replication session

detectionRules.title: Suspicious AD Replication Session — Non-DC Partner
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4932
| where DestinationDRA !contains "CN=NTDS Settings"
| project TimeGenerated, DestinationDRA, NamingContext, Options, Computer
detectionRules.notes: Legitimate replication partners have 'CN=NTDS Settings' in their DRA name. Any other value warrants investigation.

detectionRules.title: AD Replication with Unexpected Partner
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4932 NOT Destination_DRA="*CN=NTDS Settings*"
| table _time, Destination_DRA, Naming_Context, host
detectionRules.notes: Alert on replication sessions initiated with non-standard DRA names.

detectionRules.title: Suspicious Active Directory Replication from Non-DC
detectionRules.rule:
title: Suspicious Active Directory Replication from Non-DC
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4932
    filter_dc:
        DestinationDRA|contains: 'CN=NTDS Settings'
    condition: selection and not filter_dc
falsepositives:
    - New DC promotion in progress
level: high
detectionRules.notes: Detects AD replication sessions from unexpected sources.

entries.title: Synchronization of a replica of an AD naming context has ended
entries.summary: A domain controller has completed synchronizing a naming context from a source DC.
entries.details: Generated at the completion of an Active Directory naming context synchronization. Key fields: DestinationDRA, SourceDRA, NamingContext, Options, and StatusCode (0 = success). This is the completion counterpart to EID 4932. Together, 4932 and 4933 bracket the full sync cycle — the elapsed time between them and the SourceDRA identity are the critical detection fields for DCSync. A StatusCode of 0 (success) combined with SourceDRA that is not a registered DC confirms a successful DCSync credential extraction. StatusCode non-zero may indicate a failed or blocked attempt. DC-only event.

investigationPivots: StatusCode = 0 (success) + SourceDRA not a known DC = confirmed DCSync credential extraction
StatusCode non-zero may indicate a partially blocked attack — investigate SourceDRA regardless
Pair with EID 4932 to calculate sync duration — legitimate DC replication takes longer than a targeted DCSync (seconds vs. minutes for full NC sync)
SourceDRA IP address should be cross-referenced against known DC IP ranges using IPAM or DHCP records
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate DC-to-DC replication completions — will be high volume in active AD environments
Azure AD Connect sync completions if replication rights are delegated to the sync account's host

keyFields.name: DestinationDRA
keyFields.description: Destination DC completing the sync; unexpected values indicate rogue replication partner

keyFields.name: NamingContext
keyFields.description: Naming context that was synchronized

keyFields.name: AttributeCount
keyFields.description: Number of attributes synchronized; abnormally high counts may indicate bulk data exfiltration via replication

keyFields.name: SessionOperation
keyFields.description: Final operation type in the completed session

detectionRules.title: AD Replication Sync Completed with Unexpected Partner
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4933
| where DestinationDRA !contains "CN=NTDS Settings"
| project TimeGenerated, DestinationDRA, NamingContext, AttributeCount, Computer
detectionRules.notes: Complements EID 4932 — confirms a full replication cycle completed with a non-standard partner. Correlate with 4932.

detectionRules.title: AD Sync Completion with Non-Standard Partner
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4933 NOT Destination_DRA="*CN=NTDS Settings*"
| table _time, Destination_DRA, Naming_Context, Attribute_Count, host
detectionRules.notes: Alert on completed sync sessions from unexpected partners.

detectionRules.title: Active Directory Replication Sync Completed by Non-DC
detectionRules.rule:
title: Active Directory Replication Sync Completed by Non-DC
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4933
    filter_dc:
        DestinationDRA|contains: 'CN=NTDS Settings'
    condition: selection and not filter_dc
falsepositives:
    - New DC promotion in progress
level: high
detectionRules.notes: Pair with EID 4932 detection for full replication cycle coverage.

entries.title: A directory service object was modified
entries.summary: An attribute of an Active Directory object was changed.
entries.details: Generated when an attribute of an Active Directory object is modified and the object's SACL is configured to audit write operations. Key fields: SubjectAccountName (who made the change), ObjectDN (full distinguished name of the modified object), ObjectClass, AttributeLDAPDisplayName (which attribute was changed), and AttributeValue (new value). This event is extremely powerful for detecting AD object tampering — modifying the adminCount attribute to protect an account from AdminSDHolder cleanup, changing group membership via direct attribute write, modifying the msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo attribute for Kerberos delegation abuse, or altering the nTSecurityDescriptor attribute to change ACLs. Also used to detect AdminSDHolder backdoors (modifying CN=AdminSDHolder permissions) and ACL-based persistence (WriteDACL abuse). Requires SACL configuration and 'Audit Directory Service Changes' policy.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit Directory Service Changes' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > DS Access. Configure SACLs on target objects to audit 'Write' operations.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification

investigationPivots: AttributeLDAPDisplayName = 'member' on group objects reveals direct group membership manipulation (bypassing normal account management events)
AttributeLDAPDisplayName = 'msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo' or 'msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity' indicates Kerberos delegation abuse setup
ObjectDN = CN=AdminSDHolder,CN=System changes allow backdooring all protected accounts — highest severity
AttributeLDAPDisplayName = 'nTSecurityDescriptor' changes on sensitive objects (Domain Admins group, GPOs) indicate ACL backdoor creation
SubjectAccountName that is not a known admin or provisioning system making changes to Domain Admins or privileged objects requires immediate investigation
commonFalsePositives: Normal administrative operations such as user provisioning, group membership management, and attribute updates
Identity management platforms (e.g., SailPoint, Saviynt, CyberArk) that write AD attributes during lifecycle operations
Azure AD Connect performing attribute synchronization writes

keyFields.name: SubjectUserName
keyFields.description: Account that made the modification; unexpected accounts modifying privileged objects are high priority

keyFields.name: ObjectDN
keyFields.description: Distinguished name of the modified object; AdminSDHolder, privileged groups, and GPO objects are high-value targets

keyFields.name: AttributeLDAPDisplayName
keyFields.description: LDAP attribute that was changed; member (group membership), userAccountControl, adminCount are critical

keyFields.name: AttributeValue
keyFields.description: New value set on the attribute; for member changes, shows the added/removed account DN

keyFields.name: OperationType
keyFields.description: %%14674 = Value Added, %%14675 = Value Deleted

detectionRules.title: Privileged Group Membership or AdminSDHolder Modification
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 5136
| where AttributeLDAPDisplayName in ("member", "adminCount", "userAccountControl", "msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo", "servicePrincipalName")
| where ObjectDN has_any ("CN=AdminSDHolder", "CN=Domain Admins", "CN=Enterprise Admins", "CN=Schema Admins", "CN=Administrators")
| project TimeGenerated, SubjectUserName, ObjectDN, AttributeLDAPDisplayName, AttributeValue, OperationType, Computer
detectionRules.notes: Focus on high-value AD objects. Tune ObjectDN list to match your privileged group structure.

detectionRules.title: AD Privileged Object Attribute Modified
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=5136
(LDAP_Display_Name=member OR LDAP_Display_Name=adminCount OR LDAP_Display_Name=userAccountControl)
(Object_DN="*CN=AdminSDHolder*" OR Object_DN="*CN=Domain Admins*" OR Object_DN="*CN=Enterprise Admins*")
| table _time, Account_Name, Object_DN, LDAP_Display_Name, Attribute_Value, host
detectionRules.notes: Modification of privileged AD object attributes.

detectionRules.title: Privileged AD Object Modification
detectionRules.rule:
title: Privileged AD Object Modification
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 5136
        AttributeLDAPDisplayName:
            - 'member'
            - 'adminCount'
            - 'userAccountControl'
            - 'msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo'
    filter_objects:
        ObjectDN|contains:
            - 'CN=AdminSDHolder'
            - 'CN=Domain Admins'
            - 'CN=Enterprise Admins'
            - 'CN=Schema Admins'
    condition: selection and filter_objects
falsepositives:
    - Routine user provisioning
    - Scheduled admin tasks
level: high
detectionRules.notes: Tune object list to environment-specific privileged groups.

entries.title: A directory service object was created
entries.summary: A new Active Directory object was created.
entries.details: Generated when a new object is created in Active Directory and the parent container's SACL is configured to audit object creation. Key fields: SubjectAccountName (who created the object), ObjectDN (full distinguished name of the new object), and ObjectClass. This event detects creation of rogue AD objects used for persistence or privilege escalation: new user accounts in privileged OUs, new group policy objects (GPO) linked to sensitive OUs, new service principal names (SPN) on user accounts for Kerberoasting, creation of new computer objects for Kerberos delegation abuse (RBCD), and creation of new GPOs. ObjectClass = groupPolicyContainer, user, or computer in unexpected containers is high priority. Requires SACL configuration on parent containers and 'Audit Directory Service Changes' policy.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit Directory Service Changes' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > DS Access. Configure SACLs on parent containers to audit 'Create Child' operations.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification

investigationPivots: ObjectClass = groupPolicyContainer created outside normal change windows is a high-priority GPO backdoor indicator
New user objects created in Domain Admins OU, Domain Controllers OU, or other privileged containers are critical
ObjectClass = computer created by a non-standard account may indicate RBCD (Resource-Based Constrained Delegation) attack setup
SubjectAccountName should match expected provisioning accounts — creation by interactive user sessions is anomalous
commonFalsePositives: Normal user, computer, and group provisioning as part of IT operations
Identity management and provisioning platforms creating objects during onboarding workflows
GPO creation during Change Management-approved policy deployments

keyFields.name: SubjectUserName
keyFields.description: Account that created the object; unexpected accounts creating GPOs or user objects warrant investigation

keyFields.name: ObjectDN
keyFields.description: Distinguished name of the newly created object; creation in sensitive OUs is suspicious

keyFields.name: ObjectClass
keyFields.description: LDAP object class created; groupPolicyContainer (GPO), user, computer, group are common targets

entries.title: A directory service object was undeleted
entries.summary: A previously deleted Active Directory object was restored from the AD Recycle Bin.
entries.details: Generated when a deleted Active Directory object is restored (undeleted) from the AD Recycle Bin. Key fields: SubjectAccountName (who performed the restore), ObjectDN (the restored object's distinguished name), and ObjectClass. While typically a benign recovery operation, undeleting AD objects can be used by attackers to resurface previously deleted accounts or groups, recovering their original SIDs, group memberships, and potentially privileges — without triggering the standard account creation events (EID 4720). This allows privilege restoration that bypasses monitoring for new account creation. ObjectClass = user or group restorations should be verified against authorized recovery tickets. Requires AD Recycle Bin feature enabled.

investigationPivots: ObjectClass = user or group restores should be validated against IT helpdesk or change management tickets
Undeleted accounts that are immediately enabled (EID 4722) and assigned to privileged groups without provisioning tickets are high-priority
SubjectAccountName performing restores should be authorized recovery admins — recovery by interactive user accounts is unusual
commonFalsePositives: Authorized IT admins recovering accidentally deleted accounts or groups
Disaster recovery procedures restoring deleted objects as part of incident remediation

entries.title: A directory service object was moved
entries.summary: An Active Directory object was moved to a different container or OU.
entries.details: Generated when an AD object is moved from one container or organizational unit (OU) to another. Key fields: SubjectAccountName, ObjectDN (new location), OldObjectDN (previous location), and ObjectClass. Moving AD objects can have significant security implications: moving a user or computer out of a security-sensitive OU may cause Group Policy application to change, potentially reducing security controls applied to that object. Adversaries may move accounts out of tightly controlled OUs (e.g., 'Privileged Users' OU with JEA or tiering policies) into less-restricted OUs to weaken applied policies. Moving a computer account to an OU with different Group Policy can be used to disable endpoint security controls. OldObjectDN containing high-security OUs combined with movement to a less-restricted OU is suspicious.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification

investigationPivots: Objects moved from security-tiered or tightly-governed OUs to general OUs may lose applied security GPOs — review Group Policy scope change
Computer objects moved out of an OU with endpoint security GPOs (AV, EDR, logging) is a defense evasion indicator
SubjectAccountName moving objects outside of provisioning tools or change windows is anomalous
commonFalsePositives: Authorized OU restructuring during AD cleanup projects
Automated identity management tools moving objects as part of lifecycle management (e.g., moving terminated users to a disabled accounts OU)

entries.title: A directory service object was deleted
entries.summary: An Active Directory object was deleted.
entries.details: Generated when an AD object is deleted (moved to the Deleted Objects container or permanently removed if Recycle Bin is not enabled). Key fields: SubjectAccountName, ObjectDN (the deleted object's DN before deletion), and ObjectClass. Adversaries may delete AD objects as part of anti-forensics (deleting logs, evidence-related accounts) or to cause disruption (deleting computer accounts to break domain authentication). Deletion of security-critical objects — domain controller computer accounts, privileged user accounts, critical GPOs, or group objects — without change tickets is a critical finding. In ransomware scenarios, deletion of backup-related AD accounts or GPOs that manage recovery tools may precede encryption. Requires SACL configuration and 'Audit Directory Service Changes' policy.

prerequisites.description: Enable 'Audit Directory Service Changes' under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > DS Access. Configure SACLs on parent containers to audit 'Delete' operations.

investigationPivots: Deletion of domain controller, privileged user, or security-group objects without change management authorization is critical
Bulk deletion of objects (many EID 5141 events in a short window from the same SubjectAccountName) may indicate destructive ransomware pre-staging
Deletion of GPOs managing endpoint security tools (AV, EDR, patch) is a defense-evasion precursor to ransomware deployment
Cross-reference with EID 5138 after recovery to confirm object was restored and integrity verified
commonFalsePositives: Authorized deprovisioning of user, computer, or group accounts during lifecycle management
GPO cleanup removing deprecated policies as part of AD hygiene projects

keyFields.name: SubjectUserName
keyFields.description: Account that deleted the object; attacker may delete logs or cleanup created objects

keyFields.name: ObjectDN
keyFields.description: Distinguished name of the deleted object; deletion of GPOs or computer objects may indicate cleanup

keyFields.name: ObjectClass
keyFields.description: LDAP class of the deleted object
